title: CC Exercises — Commerce Core
description: Hands-on exercises covering catalogs, pricing, cart strategies, order processes, promotions, and vouchers/coupons.

sections.title: Exercise 1 — Catalog Setup, Sync & Classification
sections.content:
Objective

Create a product catalog with Staged/Online versions, add products with classification attributes, and run a synchronisation job.

Steps

  1. Create a Catalog: Via ImpEx, create a new Catalog trainingProductCatalog with two CatalogVersions: Staged and Online.
  2. Add Products in Staged: Create 5 Products in the Staged version with categories (Electronics → Laptops, Electronics → Tablets). Include names, descriptions, and images (use placeholder media).
  3. Classification System: Create a ClassificationSystem trainingClassification with a ClassificationClass PortableDevices. Add classification attributes: screenSize (number, unit: inches), weight (number, unit: grams), batteryLife (number, unit: hours). Assign your products to the classification class and set feature values.
  4. Verify in Backoffice: Open a product in Staged. Verify the classification attributes appear on the Classification tab with their values, units, and localisation.
  5. Synchronise: Run a SyncItemJob from Staged to Online. Verify using FlexibleSearch that the products now exist in both Staged and Online as separate instances with the same codes but different PKs.
  6. Test the filter: In HAC Groovy console, run a FlexibleSearch for your products without setting a session catalog version. Observe you get results from both versions. Then set the session to Online only and verify only Online products are returned.

Validation

Products exist in both versions after sync. Classification attributes are searchable. Modifying a product in Staged does not affect the Online version until the next sync.

sections.title: Exercise 2 — Europe1 Pricing with Volume Tiers
sections.content:
Objective

Configure multi-dimensional pricing with Europe1: base prices, user-group-specific prices, volume tiers, and discount rows.

Steps

  1. Base Price Rows: Via ImpEx, create PriceRows for your 5 products in EUR for all users. Set different prices for each product.
  2. User Group Pricing: Create a UserPriceGroup B2BPremium. Create additional PriceRows for 2 of your products with lower prices targeting this group. Assign a test user to this group.
  3. Volume Pricing: For one product, create 3 PriceRows with different minQty values: qty 1 = €499, qty 5 = €449, qty 20 = €399. These should all target the same product and currency.
  4. Discount Rows: Create a DiscountRow that gives 10% off all products for the B2BPremium user group.
  5. Test Resolution: In HAC Groovy console, use the Europe1PriceFactory (or CalculationService) to resolve the price for:
    • A regular user buying 1 unit → should get the base price.
    • A B2BPremium user buying 1 unit → should get the lower B2B price + 10% discount.
    • Any user buying 25 units of the volume-priced product → should get the qty-20 tier price (€399).

Validation

The specificity hierarchy works correctly: user-group prices beat generic prices, volume tiers activate at the right quantities, and discounts apply on top. Document the resolved price for each scenario and verify against manual calculation.

sections.title: Exercise 3 — Custom Add-to-Cart Strategy & Checkout
sections.content:
Objective

Override the CommerceAddToCartStrategy to add custom validation, then walk through the full checkout flow programmatically.

Steps

Part A — Custom Strategy

  1. Create CustomAddToCartStrategy extending DefaultCommerceAddToCartStrategy.
  2. Override the validateAddToCart() method: add a rule that prevents adding more than 50 units of any single product. Throw a CommerceCartModificationException with a meaningful message.
  3. Register your strategy as an alias for commerceAddToCartStrategy in your *-spring.xml.
  4. Test: try adding 60 units via HAC Groovy console using commerceCartService.addToCart(). Verify the exception is thrown. Try 30 units and verify it succeeds.

Part B — Programmatic Checkout

  1. In a HAC Groovy script, execute the full checkout flow programmatically:
    • Create or get the user's cart via cartService.
    • Add 2 products using commerceCartService.addToCart().
    • Set a delivery address using commerceCheckoutService.setDeliveryAddress().
    • Set a delivery mode using commerceCheckoutService.setDeliveryMode().
    • Set payment info using commerceCheckoutService.setPaymentInfo() (create a test PaymentInfo first).
    • Place the order using commerceCheckoutService.placeOrder().
  2. Verify the Order was created: query by order code, check its status, entries, totals, and delivery/payment info.

Validation

The custom strategy blocks excessive quantities. The programmatic checkout produces a valid Order with correct entries, prices (from Europe1), delivery cost, and status. The cart is consumed after placement.

sections.title: Exercise 4 — Promotion Rule & Coupon
sections.content:
Objective

Create a promotion rule with conditions and actions in Backoffice, create a coupon that gates a separate promotion, and test both on a cart.

Steps

Part A — Automatic Promotion

  1. In Backoffice → Marketing → Promotion Rules, create a new rule: Buy2GetFreeShipping.
  2. Condition: cart contains at least 2 items from the Electronics category.
  3. Action: free delivery (delivery cost = 0).
  4. Set priority to 100, non-exclusive. Assign to your store's promotion group.
  5. Publish the rule.
  6. Test: create a cart with 2 electronics products, set a delivery mode, calculate the cart. Verify the delivery cost is zero. Check the PromotionResult on the cart.

Part B — Coupon-Gated Promotion

  1. In Backoffice → Marketing → Coupon Management, create a Single-Code Coupon: code = SAVE15, max total redemptions = 100.
  2. Create a promotion rule: 15PercentOff.
  3. Conditions: coupon condition (referencing SAVE15) AND cart total > €200.
  4. Action: 15% percentage discount on cart.
  5. Priority: 50, non-exclusive. Publish.
  6. Test: create a cart with €300+ of products. Apply the coupon code SAVE15 to the cart. Calculate. Verify 15% discount is applied. Remove the coupon and recalculate — verify the discount disappears.
  7. Test with a cart under €200 with the coupon — verify the promotion does NOT fire (both conditions must be met).

Validation

The automatic promotion fires without user action when conditions are met. The coupon promotion fires only when both the coupon code AND the cart total condition are satisfied. PromotionResult objects on the cart accurately describe what fired and why.
